SC-001.title: First Day: What's in the System?
SC-001.description: You're on your first solo call. The homeowner says the AC isn't cooling. You hook up your gauges. What refrigerant is this?
hints: Look at the liquid pressure. R410A runs higher pressure than R22.
R410A saturation pressure at 25°C is about 225 psig.

SC-002.title: The R32 Transition
SC-002.description: New equipment install. The label says R32. Verify with gauges.
hints: R32 pressures are slightly higher than R410A at the same temperature.
Check the critical point: R32 has higher P_crit than R410A.

SC-003.title: The Overcharge
SC-003.description: System running but high power bills. Gauges look high on both sides. What's wrong?
SC-003.customer_complaint: My electric bill doubled this month.
hints: Both pressures are high. That's not a restriction.
Calculate superheat. If it's low, liquid is flooding back.

SC-004.title: The Undercharge
SC-004.description: System runs constantly but can't keep up. Suction pressure is low. What's wrong?
SC-004.customer_complaint: It never shuts off and the house is still warm.
hints: Low pressures on both sides usually means low charge or restriction.

SC-005.title: The Dirty Condenser
SC-005.description: System trips on high pressure. Reset it, check gauges. Multiple issues?
SC-005.customer_complaint: It keeps shutting off and making a loud noise.
hints: Liquid pressure over 300 psig on R410A is dangerous.
Check ambient. 35°C ambient + dirty condenser = high head pressure.

pt_chart.click_instruction: Click on curve to read values
